content.full_text:
Re
Farn Polrrtcal PnacrIcps C ouvttssloN
P.O. Box lt07 . .tllt J Strcct 'Sucramento. CA 95811-0tt07
(916) -1ll--5660 . Fax (916) -122-013tt6
December 29,2000
Edward A. Rich, President
Calaveras Olive Oil and Land
Company Inc.
P.O. Box 344
C)opperopolis, CA 95228
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-253
Dear Mr. Rich:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").l Since you do not seek advice regarding a specific
governmental decision, we can provide you only informal assistance.2 Please note that
this letter should not be taken as advice conceming any conduct that may already have
taken place. Moreover, this advice is limited to the facts presented. The Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice on duties and obligations under
the Act. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
You ask if you may participate in decisionmaking on development proposals-
submitted by the prospective purchaser of certain real property which your client had
earlier attempted to buy, under an agreement that would have paid you a commission
on the sale.
I Government Code Sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections 18109-
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c).)


FileNo. I-00-253
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the kinds of decisions you describe, so long as it is not
reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material financial effect on your economic
interests. The Act does not limit or restrict decisionmaking simply because a public
official may have earlier expressed opinions on the subject matter of a decision, or
forecast the outcome of a decision by his agency.
FACTS
You are an appointed Planning Commissioner in the 5th District of Calaveras
County, which includes the Lake Tulloch area. You are also President of Calaveras Olive
Oil and Land Company, Inc, and a licensed real estate broker who represents buyers of
olive production properties. You were contacted in 1995 by a broker from the Stockton
office of Lee & Associates. This broker was soliciting assistance in locating a buyer who
would "cash out" some or all of the limited partners of the Rancheria Del Rio Estanislaus,
a limited partnership holding 10,000 acres of real property on Lake Tulloch.
On November 9, l999,Lee & Associates submitted an offer to purchase 4,500
acres of the property held by the limited partnership, for uses consistent with the
property's current zoning. You were to receive 50o/o of arry commission on this sale.
This offer was rejected in favor of another offer at a higher price, from a buyer who
intends to develop the properfy in a manner requiring rezoning and ancillary land use
changes. This prospective buyer is now formulating development plans which, you
believe, could have a significant impact on Lake Tulloch and your district. You had told
the general partner, while he was considering the two offers, that you anticipated
regulatory difficulties for this project. The general partner seems to have construed your
observations as a threat. Your client has now purchased another property.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way using his or her official position to influence a
govemmental decision in which the official has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.)
The Calaveras County Planning Commission is a local government agency (Section
82041) and, as a planning commissioner, you are a public official within the meaning of
Section 82048. Your request for advice presupposes that you will make, or participate
in making, governmental decisions relating to land use applications submitted to the
planning commission by the prospective buyer of the Rancheria property. Section 87103
provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,


FileNo. I-00-253
Page No. 3
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any of the economic interests described as follows:3
o 
I public official has an economic interest in a bustness entity in which he or she
lras a direct or indirect investmenr a of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, fficer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. 1 (b));
. 
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has
a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
o 
I public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
primised income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
. 
{ public official has an economic interest in any source of Stfts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
o 
d pubiic official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family-this is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.s).
Regulation 18700(b) describes in detail the factors that must be shown to establish
that a publl official has a conflict of interest in a particular decision.s
As President, you have an economic interest under Section 87103(d) in the
Calaveras Olive Oil and Land Company, Inc.6 You also have an economic interest in
your personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities - that is, in personal financial effects
attributable to decisions before the planning commission. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.) We proceed, then, by asking whether any of the anticipated decisions might
' Sorne of the threshold amounts listed in Section 87103 have been double4 effective January l, 2001. On
the same date the gift limit (Section 87103(e)) will rise to $320.
n An inclirect investnent or interest means any investnent or interest owned by the spouse of an official or
by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business
entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly,
or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined at Section
82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
t Please bear in mind that the Commission has recently completed a comprehensive review of its conflict of
interest regulations, and has adopted numerous amdendments to them, which will become effective on
February l, 2001. You can review these amendments on the Commission's website, at www.fupc.ca.gov.
u We presume that the company is also a source of income to you. Although it is not clear from your
account of the facts, if you have an investment interest in the company valued at $1,000 or more, you
would have an economic interest in the company for that reason too, under Section 87103(a).


FileNo. I-00-253
Page No. 4
foreseeably have a personal financial effect on you or your business. We assume the
absence of other potentially affected economic interests, such as sources of income or
interests in real properfy situated in the vicinity of the planned project. Lee & Associates
would not be a source of income to you since the sale did not take place.
The next step requires that we determine whether your economic interests are
directly or indirectly involved in decisions before the planning commission. The subject
of these decisions is the prospective buyer's development plans for improvements to the
subject property. The prospective buyer is directly involved in any such decision, but
you and your company are only indirectly involved, assuming that you or your company
will not be initiating these proceeding, be a named party thereto, and are not the subject
of any decision. (Regulations 18704, 18704.1.)
Regulations 18705 et seq. establish the appropriate materiality standard for
financial effects on the economic interests involved in the decisions in question. The
materiality threshold for financial effects on your business, a for-profit corporation,
varies with the size of its financial assets and income. (Regulation 18705.1.) At the
least, material effects of a govemmental decision are those which will foreseeably result
in an increase or decrease of the company's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the
amount of $10,000 or more; will cause the business to incur or avoid additional expenses
or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more; or which will result in an increase or decrease in the value of its assets or liabilities
in the amount of $10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(b)(7).) Effects on your personal
finances are "material" if they amount to $250 or more within any twelve month period.
(Regulation 1 8705.5.)7
Our next question is whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that planning
commission decisions on the prospective buyer's development proposals would
materially affect your economic interests, as described above. As used here, "reasonably
foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are
substantially likely at the time of a decision is highly situation-specific. A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial
likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not "reasonably foreseeable .- (Ibid.)
Since we do not have information on specific decisions coming before the
planning commission, we cannot tell you whether a material financial effect on one of
your economic interests is "reasonably foreseeable." You will have to make this
determination for yourself as you become aware of particular decisions, basing your
judgment on all the facts and circumstances known to you at the time of the decision.
7 A financial effect on your investment interest is not considered in evaluating "personal financial effects,"
the materiality threshold for which is only $250. (Regulation 18705.5, formerly 18703.5.) A potentially
disqualifying "personal financial effect" must grow out ofeffects separate and apart from financial effects
on investment interests. Your account of the facts does not suggest to us any likelihood of a material
personal financial effect from the decisions in question.


FileNo. I-00-253
Page No. 5
If you find that a material financial effect on one of your economic interests is reasonably
foreseeable, you have a financial interest in the decision and may not make, participate
in making, or use your official position to influence that decision.s On the other hand, if
you find that you do not have a {inancial interest in a decision, you do not have a conflict
of interest, and the Act does not limit or prohibit your full participation in the
decisionmaking. This remains true even in decisions about which you have previously
expressed opinions or forecast an outcome.
If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
By
Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Commission Counsel
LTwjg
I :\Adviceltrs\0025 3.doc
8 There are two exceptions to this statement: first, if the effect of a decision on your economic interest(s) is
substantially similar to effects on the public generally or, second, if you are legally required to participate
in the decisir:n notwithstanding your financial interest. See Regulations 18700(b)(7), 18707,18700(bX8)
and 18708.
